Nudge Theory

What is a Nudge?

A nudge is a concept in behavioral economics that subtly alters the environment or context in which people make decisions with the aim of influencing their behavior. Nudges are designed to guide decisions in predictable ways by leveraging cognitive biases without restricting freedom of choice or changing incentives.

The Basic Idea

Throughout the last decade, nudging has been hard to miss in the worlds of behavioral economics and public policy. This new “hot topic” is intended to help individuals make better choices that they want to make in a predictable way while being sure to preserve their freedom of choice. Nudges (or good ones at least) are usually inexpensive or free to implement and take very little time, making them very enticing for organizational and public policy leaders looking to promote effective change! These could include something like putting healthier food choices at eye level, making public trash bins brightly colored so they’re easier to spot, or changing the default energy-use settings on appliances.

Nudges usually appeal to our System 1 brain, the mode of thinking that provides us with automatic, unconscious, and emotional responses to stimuli. System 1 often leads us to outcomes that may not be favorable to ourselves, others, or even our planet in the long run—such as impulse shopping, alcohol addiction, or choosing the first thing on the menu, even when it’s not a particularly healthy or ethical choice. Understanding the power we have to efficiently and effectively ‘nudge’ our System 1 brains, which make thousands of micro-decisions for us on a daily basis, into healthier, safer, and more sustainable choices has been a booming field of research in recent years.

Some examples of common nudges include:

“A nudge… is any aspect of the choice architecture that alters people’s behavior in a predictable way without forbidding any options or significantly changing their economic incentives. To count as a mere nudge, the intervention must be easy and cheap to avoid. Nudges are not mandates. Putting the fruit at eye level counts as a nudge. Banning junk food does not.”

Richard Thaler and Cass Sunstein, the behavioral economists behind Nudge Theory1

About the Author

Annika Steele

Annika completed her Masters at the London School of Economics in an interdisciplinary program combining behavioral science, behavioral economics, social psychology, and sustainability. Professionally, she’s applied data-driven insights in project management, consulting, data analytics, and policy proposal. Passionate about the power of psychology to influence an array of social systems, her research has looked at reproductive health, animal welfare, and perfectionism in female distance runners.

--- SOURCE 2: Nudge - BehavioralEconomics.com | The BE Hub ---
URL: https://www.behavioraleconomics.com/resources/mini-encyclopedia-of-be/nudge/

SUMMARY:
A nudge is any aspect of the choice architecture that alters people's behavior in a predictable way without forbidding any options.

FULL CONTENT:
![X](https://www.behavioraleconomics.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/11/X_logo_2023_white-3-2.png)
![BehavioralEconomics.com | The BE Hub Logo](https://www.behavioraleconomics.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/08/BE_medium_360x60_.png)
![BehavioralEconomics.com | The BE Hub Logo](https://www.behavioraleconomics.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/08/BE-Icon-64x64_.png)
![BehavioralEconomics.com | The BE Hub Logo](https://www.behavioraleconomics.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/08/BE_medium_360x60_textV3_.png)

# Nudge

According to Thaler and Sunstein (2008, p. 6), a nudge is

any aspect of the choice architecture that alters people’s behavior in a predictable way without forbidding any options or significantly changing their economic incentives. To count as a mere nudge, the intervention must be easy and cheap to avoid. Nudges are not mandates. Putting the fruit at eye level counts as a nudge. Banning junk food does not.

Perhaps the most frequently mentioned nudge is the setting of [defaults](https://www.behavioraleconomics.com/default-optionsetting/), which are pre-set courses of action that take effect if nothing is specified by the decision-maker. This type of nudge, which works with a human tendency for inaction, appears to be particularly successful, as people may stick with a choice for many years (Gill, 2018).

On a cost-adjusted basis, the effectiveness of nudges is often greater than that of traditional approaches (Benartzi et al., 2017), There is a growing body of research on the effectiveness of different kinds of nudges (Mertens et al., 2022), suggesting that nudges have at least a small effect on behavior (DellaVigna & Linos, 2022). However, some academics have argued that correcting for the presence of publication bias may eliminate the published effectiveness of nudges entirely (Maier et al., 2022).

Questions about the theoretical and practical value of nudging have been explored (Kosters & Van der Heijden, 2015) with respect to their ability to produce lasting behavior change (Frey & Rogers, 2014), as well as their assumptions of irrationality and lack of agency (Gigerenzer, 2015). There may also be limits to nudging due to non-cognitive constraints and population differences, such as a lack of financial resources if nudges are designed to increase savings (Loibl et al., 2016). Limits in the application of nudges speak to the value of experimentation in order to test behavioral interventions prior to their implementation.

As a complementary approach that addresses the shortcomings of nudges, Hertwig and Grüne-Yanoff (2017) propose the concept of boosts, a decision-making aid that fosters people’s competence to make informed choices.

(See also [choice architecture](https://www.behavioraleconomics.com/choice-architecture/).)

### Learn More

More about nudging on [this website](https://www.behavioraleconomics.com/?s=nudging).

Find the [latest research](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=nudging) on nudging.

[Watch videos](https://www.google.com/search?q=nudging&tbm=vid) on nudging.

### References

Benartzi, S., Beshears, J., Milkman, K. L., Sunstein, C. R., Thaler, R. H., Shankar, M., Tucker-Ray, W., Congdon, W. J., & Galing, S. (2017). Should governments invest more in nudging?. *Psychological Science, 28*(8), 1041-1055.

DellaVigna, S., & Linos, E. (2022). RCTs to scale: Comprehensive evidence from two nudge units. *Econometrica*, *90*(1), 81-116.

Frey, E., & Rogers, T. (2014). Persistence: How treatment effects persist after interventions stop. *Policy Insights from the Behavioral and Brain Sciences, 1*(1), 172-179.

Gigerenzer, G. (2015). On the supposed evidence for libertarian paternalism. *Review of Philosophy and Psychology, 6,* 361-383.

Gill, D. (2018, February 20). When ‘nudging’ is forever—the case of Sweden. *Chicago Booth Review[.](http://review.chicagobooth.edu/behavioral-science/2018/article/when-nudging-forever-case-sweden)* <http://review.chicagobooth.edu/behavioral-science/2018/article/when-nudging-forever-case-sweden>.

Hertwig, R., & Grüne-Yanoff, T. (2017). Nudging and boosting: Steering or empowering good decisions. *Perspectives on Psychological Science*, *12*(6), 973-986.

Kosters, M., & Van der Heijden, J. (2015). From mechanism to virtue: Evaluating Nudge theory. *Evaluation, 21*(3), 276-291.

Loibl, C., Jones, L. E., Haisley, E., & Loewenstein, G. (2016). *Testing strategies to increase saving and retention in individual development account programs.* http://dx.doi.org/10.2139/ssrn.2735625.

Maier, M., Bartoš, F., Stanley, T. D., Shanks, D. R., Harris, A. J., & Wagenmakers, E. J. (2022). No evidence for nudging after adjusting for publication bias. *Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences*, *119*(31), e2200300119.

Mertens, S., Herberz, M., Hahnel, U. J. J. , & Brosch, T. (2022). The effectiveness of nudging: A meta-analysis of choice architecture interventions across behavioral domains. *PNAS.* [https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.2107346118.](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.2107346118)

Thaler, R. H., & Sunstein, C. (2008). *Nudge: Improving decisions about health, wealth, and happiness.* New Haven, CT: Yale University Press.

SUMMARY:
Nudge theory - Wikipedia Nudge theory Nudge theory **Nudge theory** is a concept in behavioral economics, decision making, behavioral policy, social psychology, consumer behavior, and related behavioral sciences that proposes adaptive designs of the decision environment (choice architecture) as ways to influence the behavior and decision-making of groups or individuals. Nudges > A nudge, as we will use the term, is any aspect of the choice architecture that alters people's behavior in a predictable way without forbidding any options or significantly changing their economic incentives. **^**Marques, Osiris Ricardo Bezerra; de Souza Neto, Valério Rodrigues (2022-07-28), "Behavioural Nudging", _Encyclopedia of Tourism Management and Marketing_, Edward Elgar Publishing, doi "Doi (identifier)"):10.4337/9781800377486.behavioural.nudging, ISBN "ISBN (identifier)")978-1-80037-747-9, S2CID "S2CID (identifier)")246764759, retrieved 2022-08-25 *   Nudge theory

*   [Social and Behavioral Sciences Team](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Social_and_Behavioral_Sciences_Team "Social and Behavioral Sciences Team") (US)

Related concepts

*   [Behavioral economics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Behavioral_economics "Behavioral economics")
*   [Choice architecture](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Choice_architecture "Choice architecture")
*   [Default effect](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Default_effect "Default effect")
*   [Design for behaviour change](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Design_for_behaviour_change "Design for behaviour change")
*   [Libertarian paternalism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Libertarian_paternalism "Libertarian paternalism")
*   [Paternalism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Paternalism "Paternalism")
*   [Social engineering](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Social_engineering_(political_science) "Social engineering (political science)")
*   [Social proof](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Social_proof "Social proof")
*   [IT-backed authoritarianism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/IT-backed_authoritarianism "IT-backed authoritarianism")
*   [v](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Template:Nudge_Theory "Template:Nudge Theory")
*   [t](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Template_talk:Nudge_Theory "Template talk:Nudge Theory")
*   [e](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Special:EditPage/Template:Nudge_Theory "Special:EditPage/Template:Nudge Theory")

**Nudge theory** is a concept in [behavioral economics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Behavioral_economics "Behavioral economics"), [decision making](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Decision_making "Decision making"), behavioral policy, [social psychology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Social_psychology "Social psychology"), [consumer behavior](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Consumer_behavior "Consumer behavior"), and related [behavioral sciences](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Behavioral_sciences "Behavioral sciences")[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-Carsta-1)[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-2)[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-3)[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-4) that proposes adaptive designs of the decision environment ([choice architecture](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Choice_architecture "Choice architecture")) as ways to [influence](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Social_influence "Social influence") the behavior and [decision-making](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Decision_making "Decision making") of groups or individuals. Nudging contrasts with other ways to achieve compliance, such as [education](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Education "Education"), [legislation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Legislation "Legislation") or [enforcement](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Enforcement "Enforcement").[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-5)

The **nudge** concept was popularized in the 2008 book _[Nudge: Improving Decisions About Health, Wealth, and Happiness](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge\_(book) "Nudge (book)")_, by behavioral economist [Richard Thaler](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Richard_Thaler "Richard Thaler") and legal scholar [Cass Sunstein](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cass_Sunstein "Cass Sunstein"), two American scholars at the [University of Chicago](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Chicago "University of Chicago"). It has influenced British and American politicians. Several nudge units exist around the world at the national level (UK, Germany, Japan, and others) as well as at the international level (e.g. [World Bank](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank"), [UN](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations"), and the [European Commission](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/European_Commission "European Commission")).[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-6) There is ongoing debate over whether "nudge theory" is a recent novel development in behavioral economics or merely a new term for one of many methods for influencing behavior.[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-Carsta-1)[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-Why_a_nudge_is_not_enough:_A_social-7)

There have been some controversies regarding effectiveness of nudges. Maier et al. wrote that, after correcting the [publication bias](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Publication_bias "Publication bias") found by Mertens et al. (2021), there is no evidence that nudging would have any effect.[[8]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-Maier-8) "Nudging" is an umbrella term referring to many techniques, and skeptics believe some nudges (e.g. default effect) can be highly effective while others have little to no effect, and call for future work that shift away from investigating average effects but focus on moderators instead.[[9]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-9) A meta-analysis of all unpublished nudging studies carried by nudge units with over 23 million individuals in the United Kingdom and United States found support for many nudges, but with substantially weaker effects than effects found in published studies.[[10]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-10) Moreover, some researchers criticized the "one-nudge-for-all" approach and advocated for more studies and implementations of personalized nudging (based on individual differences), which appear to be substantially more effective, with a more robust and consistent evidence base.[[11]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-11)[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-12)

Nudges
------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Nudge_theory&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Nudges")]

### Definition

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Nudge_theory&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Definition")]

The first formulation of the term _nudge_ and associated principles was developed in [cybernetics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cybernetics "Cybernetics") by [James Wilk](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=James_Wilk&action=edit&redlink=1 "James Wilk (page does not exist)") before 1995 and described by [Brunel University](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brunel_University_London "Brunel University London") academic [D. J. Stewart](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=D._J._Stewart_(academic)&action=edit&redlink=1 "D. J. Stewart (academic) (page does not exist)") as "the art of the nudge" (sometimes referred to as _micronudges_).[[13]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-13) It also drew on methodological influences from clinical [psychotherapy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Psychotherapy "Psychotherapy") tracing back to [Gregory Bateson](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gregory_Bateson "Gregory Bateson"), including contributions from [Milton Erickson](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Milton_Erickson "Milton Erickson"), [Watzlawick](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Paul_Watzlawick "Paul Watzlawick"), [Weakland](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/John_Weakland "John Weakland") and Fisch, and Bill O'Hanlon.[[14]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-14) In this variant, the nudge is a [microtargeted](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Microtargeting "Microtargeting") design geared toward a specific group of people, irrespective of the scale of intended intervention.

In 2008, [Richard Thaler](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Richard_Thaler "Richard Thaler") and [Cass Sunstein](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cass_Sunstein "Cass Sunstein")'s book _[Nudge: Improving Decisions About Health, Wealth, and Happiness](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge\_(book) "Nudge (book)")_ brought nudge theory to prominence.[[15]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-15)[[16]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-Kosters-16) The authors refer to the influencing of behaviour without coercion as _[libertarian paternalism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Libertarian\_paternalism "Libertarian paternalism")_ and the influencers as _[choice architects](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Choice\_architecture "Choice architecture")_.[[17]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-speak-17)[[18]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-18)

Thaler and Sunstein defined their concept as the following:[[19]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-R._Thaler_and_C._Sunstein._2008-19): 6

> A nudge, as we will use the term, is any aspect of the [choice architecture](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Choice_architecture "Choice architecture") that alters people's behavior in a predictable way without forbidding any options or significantly changing their economic incentives. To count as a mere nudge, the intervention must be easy and cheap to avoid. Nudges are not mandates. Putting fruit at eye level counts as a nudge. Banning junk food does not.

In this form, drawing on [behavioral economics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Behavioral_economics "Behavioral economics"), the nudge is more generally applied in order to influence behaviour.

One of the most frequently cited examples of a nudge is the etching of the [image of a housefly into the men's room urinals](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Urinal_target "Urinal target") at Amsterdam's [Schiphol Airport](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Schiphol_Airport "Schiphol Airport"), which is intended to "improve the aim."[[19]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-R._Thaler_and_C._Sunstein._2008-19)

[![Image 4](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Urinal_FLy2.jpg/250px-Urinal_FLy2.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Urinal_FLy2.jpg)

A [fly image at the bottom of a urinal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Urinal_target "Urinal target") has been proven to improve men's aim, leading to lowered cleaning costs.[[20]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-20)

The book also gained a following among US and UK politicians, as well as in the private sector and in [public health](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Public_health "Public health").[[21]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-21)

### Overview

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Nudge_theory&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Overview")]

A nudge makes it more likely that an individual will make a particular choice, or behave in a particular way, by altering the environment so that automatic [cognitive processes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cognitive_processes "Cognitive processes") are triggered to favour the desired outcome.[[22]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-Saghai-22)[[23]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-ReferenceA-23)

An individual's behaviour is not always in alignment with their intentions (a discrepancy known as a _[value-action gap](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Value-action\_gap "Value-action gap")_).[[23]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-ReferenceA-23) It is common knowledge that humans are not fully [rational](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rationality "Rationality") beings; that is, people will often do something that is not in their own self-interest, even when they are aware that their actions are not in their best interest.[[16]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-Kosters-16) As an example, when hungry, people who diet often underestimate their ability to lose weight, and their intentions to eat healthy can be temporarily weakened until they are satiated.[[24]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-24)

Nobel Laureate [Daniel Kahneman](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Daniel_Kahneman "Daniel Kahneman") describes two distinct systems for processing information as to why people sometimes act against their own self-interest: System 1 is fast, automatic, and highly susceptible to environmental influences; System 2 processing is slow, reflective, and takes into account explicit goals and intentions.[[22]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-Saghai-22) When situations are overly complex or overwhelming for an individual's cognitive capacity, or when an individual is faced with time-constraints or other pressures, System 1 processing takes over decision-making.[[23]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-ReferenceA-23)[[25]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-ReferenceB-25) System 1 processing relies on various judgmental heuristics to make decisions, resulting in faster decisions.[[25]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-ReferenceB-25) Unfortunately, this can also lead to suboptimal decisions. In fact, Thaler and Sunstein[[19]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-R._Thaler_and_C._Sunstein._2008-19) trace [maladaptive behaviour](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Maladaptive_behavior "Maladaptive behavior") to situations in which System 1 processing overrides an individual's explicit values and goals.[[23]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-ReferenceA-23) It is well documented that habitual behaviour is resistant to change without a disruption to the environmental cues that trigger that behaviour.[[25]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-ReferenceB-25)

Nudging techniques aim to use [judgmental heuristics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heuristics_in_judgment_and_decision-making "Heuristics in judgment and decision-making") to the advantage of the party that is creating the set of choices. In other words, a nudge alters the environment so that when heuristic, or System 1, decision-making is used, the resulting choice will be the most positive or desired outcome.[[25]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-ReferenceB-25) An example of such a nudge is switching the placement of junk food in a store, so that fruit and other healthy options are located next to the cash register, while junk food is relocated to another part of the store.[[26]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-ReferenceC-26)

### Techniques

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Nudge_theory&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Techniques")]

Nudges are small changes in the environment that are easy and inexpensive to implement.[[22]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-Saghai-22) In head-to-head comparisons, randomized experiments have that nudges can sometimes motivate behavior change more effectively than paying people.[[27]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-27)[[28]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-28) Several different techniques exist for nudging, including [defaults](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Default_effect "Default effect"), [social-proof](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Social_proof "Social proof") heuristics, and increasing the salience of the desired option.

A _[default option](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Default\_option "Default option")_ is the option that person automatically receives for doing nothing. People are more likely to choose a particular option if it is the default option.[[25]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-ReferenceB-25) For example, Pichert and Katsikopoulos (2008) found that a greater number of consumers chose the [renewable energy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Renewable_energy "Renewable energy") option for electricity when it was offered as the default option.[[29]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-29) Similarly, the default options given to mobile apps developers in advertising networks can significantly impact consumers' privacy.[[30]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-30)

A _social-proof heuristic_ refers to the tendency of people to look at the behavior of others to help guide their own behavior. Studies have found some success in using social-proof heuristics to nudge people to make healthier food choices.[[31]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-31)

When people's attention is drawn toward a particular option, that option will become more salient and they will be more likely to choose it. As an example, in [snack](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Snack "Snack") shops at train stations in the Netherlands, consumers purchased more fruit and [healthy snack](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Healthy_diet "Healthy diet") options when they were relocated next to the cash register.[[26]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-ReferenceC-26) Since then, other similar studies have been made regarding the placement of healthier food options close to the checkout counter and the effect on the consuming behavior of the customers, and this is now considered an effective and well-accepted nudge.[[32]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-32)

Application
-----------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Nudge_theory&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Application")]

Behavioral insights and nudges are currently used in many countries around the world.[[33]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-33)[[34]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-34)

### Government

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Nudge_theory&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Government")]

[![Image 5](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Stimmzettel-Anschluss.jpg/250px-Stimmzettel-Anschluss.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Stimmzettel-Anschluss.jpg)

In the [1938 Austrian Anschluss referendum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1938_Austrian_Anschluss_referendum "1938 Austrian Anschluss referendum"),[[35]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nudge_theory#cite_note-Sammut-35) the larger ce

SUMMARY:
Nudging applies choice architecture principles to influence decisions subtly, encouraging beneficial behaviors while maintaining autonomy. Nudge theory is a concept within behavioral economics that seeks to influence people’s decisions and behaviors by subtly changing the context or environment in which choices are presented. Effective choice architecture encourages people to make the best decision for their well-being by subtly guiding their behavior. Nudges exploit this bias to encourage positive behaviors by making beneficial choices the default. Nudging, backed by decades of research in behavioral economics, demonstrates how small interventions can drive significant positive change in decision-making. By leveraging choice architecture, addressing human tendencies, and using tools like default options, organizations can encourage beneficial behaviors in diverse areas such as health, finance, and sustainability.

Nudging: How Behavioral Economics Can Transform Practices
=========================================================

April 24, 2025•[Voltage Control](https://voltagecontrol.com/blog/author/voltage-control/)

[SUBSCRIBE →](https://voltagecontrol.com/newsletter/)

Table of contents
-----------------

*   [What Is Nudge Theory?](https://voltagecontrol.com/articles/nudging-how-behavioral-economics-can-transform-practices/#h-what-is-nudge-theory)
*   [Core Concepts of Behavioral Nudging](https://voltagecontrol.com/articles/nudging-how-behavioral-economics-can-transform-practices/#h-core-concepts-of-behavioral-nudging)
    *   [1. Choice Architecture](https://voltagecontrol.com/articles/nudging-how-behavioral-economics-can-transform-practices/#h-1-choice-architecture)
    *   [2. Default Options](https://voltagecontrol.com/articles/nudging-how-behavioral-economics-can-transform-practices/#h-2-default-options)
    *   [3. Mental Accounting](https://voltagecontrol.com/articles/nudging-how-behavioral-economics-can-transform-practices/#h-3-mental-accounting)
    *   [4. Status Quo Bias](https://voltagecontrol.com/articles/nudging-how-behavioral-economics-can-transform-practices/#h-4-status-quo-bias)
    *   [5. Heuristic Tendencies](https://voltagecontrol.com/articles/nudging-how-behavioral-economics-can-transform-practices/#h-5-heuristic-tendencies)

*   [Applications of Nudging in Various Fields](https://voltagecontrol.com/articles/nudging-how-behavioral-economics-can-transform-practices/#h-applications-of-nudging-in-various-fields)
    *   [1. Health and Wellness](https://voltagecontrol.com/articles/nudging-how-behavioral-economics-can-transform-practices/#h-1-health-and-wellness)
    *   [2. Energy Conservation](https://voltagecontrol.com/articles/nudging-how-behavioral-economics-can-transform-practices/#h-2-energy-conservation)
    *   [3. Financial Decision-Making](https://voltagecontrol.com/articles/nudging-how-behavioral-economics-can-transform-practices/#h-3-financial-decision-making)
    *   [4. Education and Public Participation](https://voltagecontrol.com/articles/nudging-how-behavioral-economics-can-transform-practices/#h-4-education-and-public-participation)

*   [Nudging in Practice: Tools and Strategies](https://voltagecontrol.com/articles/nudging-how-behavioral-economics-can-transform-practices/#h-nudging-in-practice-tools-and-strategies)
*   [Conclusion](https://voltagecontrol.com/articles/nudging-how-behavioral-economics-can-transform-practices/#h-conclusion)
*   [FAQs](https://voltagecontrol.com/articles/nudging-how-behavioral-economics-can-transform-practices/#h-faqs)

In recent decades, behavioral economics has provided groundbreaking insights into decision-making, particularly how people can be influenced to make better choices without limiting their freedom of choice. Central to this field is the concept of nudging, developed by Richard H. Thaler and Cass R. Sunstein, which uses small interventions to guide human behavior toward more optimal outcomes in health, finance, energy, and beyond. Nudging applies choice architecture principles to influence decisions subtly, encouraging beneficial behaviors while maintaining autonomy.

In this article, we explore the origins of nudging, its core principles, and real-world applications across various fields, demonstrating its potential to transform individual and [organizational practices](https://voltagecontrol.com/private-certification-programs/).

**What Is Nudge Theory?**
-------------------------

[Nudge theory](https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/strategy-and-corporate-finance/our-insights/much-anew-about-nudging) is a concept within behavioral economics that seeks to influence people’s decisions and behaviors by subtly changing the context or environment in which choices are presented. Instead of restricting options or enforcing compliance, nudges work by aligning human tendencies with desired outcomes.

For example, instead of forcing individuals to save for retirement, organizations use default options such as automatic enrollment in savings plans. This leverages natural heuristic tendencies, such as sticking with the status quo bias, to encourage better financial decisions.

Key characteristics of nudges include:

*   **Maintaining freedom of choice**: Nudges do not restrict options or penalize individuals for their decisions.
*   **Simplifying decision-making**: By reducing complexity, nudges cater to the limited cognitive capacity of individuals.
*   **Targeting irrational choices**: Nudges address predictable patterns of irrational choices, ensuring decisions are more aligned with long-term well-being.

![Image 6](blob:http://localhost/01168d954e672aa1249f5e9fcf08214a)
**Core Concepts of Behavioral Nudging**
---------------------------------------

Behavioral nudging is built on several foundational concepts that shape its effectiveness. These principles leverage psychological and cognitive insights to subtly guide behavior while preserving individual autonomy. Below are the core concepts that underpin nudging strategies:

### **1. Choice Architecture**

The way choices are structured and presented to individuals significantly impacts their decisions. Effective choice architecture encourages people to make the best decision for their well-being by subtly guiding their behavior. Examples include redesigning cafeteria layouts to highlight healthier options or presenting energy usage comparisons to encourage energy conservation.

### **2. Default Options**

One of the most powerful tools of nudging, default options, capitalizes on people’s tendency to accept the status quo. For instance:

*   Automatically enrolling employees in retirement savings plans increases participation rates and improves savings rates.
*   Setting energy-efficient appliances as the default setting reduces energy consumption and promotes environmental sustainability.

### **3. Mental Accounting**

Mental accounting, a concept introduced by Richard Thaler, refers to how individuals categorize and evaluate financial decisions. For example, framing energy savings in terms of monthly monetary savings rather than kilowatts is more effective in reducing consumption.

### **4. Status Quo Bias**

People often prefer maintaining the current state of affairs, even when change would be beneficial. Nudges exploit this bias to encourage positive behaviors by making beneficial choices the default.

### **5. Heuristic Tendencies**

Humans often rely on mental shortcuts or heuristics to make decisions. While these can lead to irrational choices, nudges help counteract negative outcomes by simplifying complex decisions and encouraging positive actions.

**Applications of Nudging in Various Fields**
---------------------------------------------

Nudging has become a versatile tool across industries, demonstrating measurable success in fostering beneficial behaviors.

### **1. Health and Wellness**

Nudges are widely used to encourage healthier lifestyles and better food choices:

*   Healthy options are prominently displayed in cafeterias, vending machines, and supermarkets, making them the easiest choice.
*   Warning labels on unhealthy options, such as sugary drinks or processed snacks, deter consumption by emphasizing the health risks.
*   Interactive fitness apps use nudges like notifications and streak rewards to encourage regular exercise.

### **2. Energy Conservation**

Nudging has proven effective in reducing energy consumption and promoting sustainability:

*   Comparative energy reports allow households to see how their usage compares to their neighbors, tapping into relational responding and social competition.
*   Smart thermostats are programmed with default options that optimize energy use, such as lowering temperatures during unoccupied hours.

### **3. Financial Decision-Making**

In finance, nudging has significantly increased savings rates and financial stability:

*   Automatic enrollment in employer-sponsored retirement plans helps employees save without requiring active decision-making.
*   Budgeting apps use mental accounting by categorizing expenses, encouraging better financial habits.

### **4. Education and Public Participation**

In education and public services, nudges have increased engagement and participation rates:

*   Personalized reminders about school registration deadlines or public service renewals nudge people to act promptly.
*   Simplifying application processes for programs like financial aid ensures higher completion rates.

![Image 8](blob:http://localhost/36aa7b803ba2307d5413ef234931ac70)
**Nudging in Practice: Tools and Strategies**
---------------------------------------------

Behavioral scientists have developed a robust set of tools to implement nudging effectively. These include:

1.   **Framing Effects**:

How information is presented can shape decisions. For example, framing a choice as a loss (“Save $100 by reducing energy use!”) is often more persuasive than a gain (“Earn $100 in savings!”). This is rooted in prospect theory developed by Daniel Kahneman.

1.   **Convenience Enhancements**:

Making the desired behavior easier or more accessible increases compliance. Examples include:

*   Installing recycling bins in high-traffic areas.
*   Creating user-friendly digital interfaces for government or healthcare services.

1.   **Relational Responding**:

Leveraging comparisons and social feedback (e.g., “You are using 20% less energy than your neighbors!”) to motivate positive behavior.

1.   **Functional Properties**:

Nudges should align with individuals’ everyday habits and routines to ensure consistent effectiveness.

**Conclusion**
--------------

Nudging, backed by decades of research in behavioral economics, demonstrates how small interventions can drive significant positive change in decision-making. By leveraging choice architecture, addressing human tendencies, and using tools like default options, organizations can encourage beneficial behaviors in diverse areas such as health, finance, and sustainability.

Voltage Control’s [certification programs](https://voltagecontrol.com/facilitation-certification/) empower leaders to understand and apply these principles effectively, fostering collaboration and innovation. Transform your organization’s decision-making practices with expert facilitation and change management training.

**FAQs**
--------

*   **What is behavioral economics, and how does it relate to nudging?**

Behavioral economics combines principles of economic theory and psychology to understand how people make decisions. Nudging applies this knowledge to design environments that promote positive behaviors without limiting freedom of choice.

*   **How does choice architecture influence behavior?**

Choice architects structure decisions to make beneficial behaviors more likely. For example, presenting healthy options more prominently in cafeterias encourages healthier eating habits.

*   **What role do default options play in behavioral nudging?**

Default options capitalize on people’s tendency to stick with the status quo bias, increasing participation rates in programs like retirement savings or organ donation.

*   **What is mental accounting, and how does it work?**

Mental accounting refers to how individuals categorize and evaluate financial resources. By framing savings in relatable terms, such as daily cost reductions, it encourages smarter spending and saving habits.

*   **What industries can benefit from nudging?**

Nudging has been successfully applied in healthcare, education, finance, environmental conservation, and public policy to drive positive outcomes and increase efficiency.

A nudge is a technique used by choice architects in order to change someone's behaviour in a very easy and low-cost way, without reducing the number of choices

enriched_points.expanded_content: Nudging is a behavioral economics concept that subtly influences decision-making by modifying the environment, guiding choices without restricting freedom or changing economic incentives.
Nudges operate by leveraging cognitive biases and heuristics, encouraging better decision-making through simple changes in choice architecture, thus making desired options more accessible.
Successful nudges include placing healthier foods at eye level in stores, default enrollment in retirement plans, and using social comparisons to promote energy conservation.
Nudging offers cost-effective, non-intrusive ways to promote beneficial behaviors, improving public health, financial stability, and environmental sustainability without mandating compliance.
